name: programming
tagline: Prescriptive strict-typing/TDD coding-standards skill for Python, Rust, TypeScript, and Go, gating edits behind mandatory reference reads.
verdict: No security red flags: no credential access, network egress, shell-piped remote code, obfuscation, or hidden instructions. It's a legitimate, if very opinionated, engineering style guide. Hygiene gaps: no version/changelog field, no explicit 'when NOT to use' guidance, and a long keyword-stuffed description that risks over-triggering and adds context overhea

highlights.summary: No negative-trigger guidance anywhere (e.g. other languages, non-code docs, config-only edits); the skill instead insists on 'no exceptions for small or one-off code', leaving no stated boundary where

Mandates reading full README plus 'every file under references/... that the README tells you to load on demand' before writing any code, explicitly even for 'small or one-off' scripts — heavy, per-tas
Description is extremely long and keyword-stuffed (near 2000 chars, dozens of comma-separated trigger terms plus 'MUST USE for ANY work'), optimized for maximal matching rather than concise clarity —
